title: Safe Administration of a HIGH-ALERT Continuous IV Heparin Infusion
subtitle: A 64-year-old man with an acute pulmonary embolism is started on a weight-based IV unfractionated heparin infusion. The nurse must run this high-alert drug safely: independent double-checks, protocol-based aPTT titration, bleeding and HIT surveillance.

patient.name: Raymond Hill
patient.admitDate: Admitted today with acute submassive pulmonary embolism. Started on a weight-based IV unfractionated heparin (UFH) infusion per the institutional nomogram. Hemodynamically stable on 2 L nasal cannula. No active bleeding.
patient.room: Telemetry; smart-pump heparin infusion, labs per protocol

labs.r: Hold/decrease per protocol; bleeding risk up.

nursesNote: Raymond Hill, 64, acute submassive PE, started on weight-based IV unfractionated heparin per nomogram. Stable on 2 L. Smart-pump infusion. 6-hour aPTT is 95 sec (goal 60–80) — supratherapeutic. No active bleeding. Platelets and Hgb being trended; protamine available as reversal agent.
providerOrders: IV unfractionated heparin per weight-based nomogram; titrate to aPTT goal 60–80 sec
aPTT every 6 hours until therapeutic ×2, then daily; CBC/platelets daily
Independent double-check by 2 RNs for initiation and every rate change
Bleeding precautions; protamine sulfate available as antidote
Notify provider for active bleeding or platelet drop ≥50%

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What does safe high-alert heparin require?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate statements about starting this infusion safely.

opts.text: Heparin is a HIGH-ALERT drug needing an independent double-check of pump settings
The dose is WEIGHT-BASED per the institutional protocol/nomogram
opts.rat: Bolus and rate follow the nomogram.

opts.text: aPTT (or anti-Xa) guides titration of the infusion
Baseline aPTT, PT/INR, platelets, and Hgb are drawn before starting
A single-nurse check is enough because heparin is low-risk
opts.rat: False — it is high-alert; two-nurse verify.

opts.text: 'Units' labels and concentrations must be verified to avoid an insulin/heparin mix-up
opts.rat: Both dose in units — verify carefully.

opts.text: An aPTT of 95 sec (goal 60–80) is supratherapeutic and raises bleeding risk
Per protocol, hold or decrease the infusion and recheck the aPTT
A ~40–50% platelet drop around day 4–10 raises concern for HIT
A supratherapeutic aPTT alone means give protamine routinely
opts.rat: Protamine is for significant bleeding/overdose, not a high aPTT alone.

opts.text: A falling hemoglobin could signal occult bleeding
Continue the same rate regardless of the aPTT result

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: The 6-hour aPTT is critically high at 95 sec

opts.text: Assess the patient for active bleeding (neuro, GI, urine, skin, vitals)
Hold or decrease the infusion per the protocol/nomogram
Notify the provider and verify the corrected rate with a double-check
Recheck the aPTT at the protocol-specified interval
Document the event and the rate change

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Classify each heparin-infusion practice as Safe or Unsafe.

opts.text: Independent double-check of the bag, concentration, and pump rate
Use a smart pump with dose-error-reduction limits
Titrate strictly per the weight-based protocol and aPTT/anti-Xa
Keep protamine sulfate available as the reversal agent
Monitor platelets for HIT and hemoglobin for bleeding
Teach bleeding precautions and to report any bleeding
Adjust the rate from memory without the protocol
Give a heparin IV push to 'catch up' a subtherapeutic level
Skip the second-nurse verification because the unit is busy
Confuse the 'units' with insulin or use the wrong concentration

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Provider increases the rate after a subtherapeutic aPTT — sequence it
steps.inst: Rank the order of safe actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Verify the new order against the weight-based protocol
Obtain an independent double-check of the new setting with a second nurse
Reprogram the smart pump and confirm concentration and rate
Reassess the patient and document the change
Recheck the aPTT at the protocol interval
Two nurses independently verified the bag and pump rate before the change
The nurse held the infusion for the supratherapeutic aPTT per protocol
Platelets were trended and the provider notified of a ~40% drop
Bleeding precautions were taught and the patient verbalized them
The aPTT was rechecked at the ordered interval
The nurse gave a heparin bolus from memory to reach goal faster
The second-nurse check was skipped 'to save time'
A supratherapeutic aPTT was ignored and the rate left unchanged
Protamine was given routinely for a high aPTT without bleeding

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
